summary:
The Carnac stone alignments in the Morbihan region of Brittany, France, constitute the largest concentration of prehistoric megalithic monuments in the world. Consisting of over 3,000 standing stones arranged in multiple parallel rows stretching for several kilometers across the landscape, the Carnac alignments were erected by Neolithic farming communities beginning around 4500 BCE and potentially continuing over centuries. They form part of the broader [[event:35|megalithic culture of western Europe]] and represent one of its most spectacular expressions.

The alignments are organized into three main groups — Le Ménec, Kermario, and Kerlescan — each consisting of multiple parallel rows of menhirs (standing stones) ranging from less than a meter to several meters in height. The stones decrease in size from one end to the other, suggesting intentional planning. Several stone enclosures or cromlechs are associated with the alignments. The purpose of the structures has been much debated: astronomical functions (tracking solstices or lunar cycles), ceremonial processional routes, territorial markers, and monuments to ancestral cult have all been proposed, and the true function may have involved several of these purposes simultaneously.

The Carnac alignments represent an enormous investment of community labor in landscape modification and monument building, reflecting the organized social capacity of Neolithic Breton communities. As a UNESCO World Heritage Site candidate and one of the most visited prehistoric sites in Europe, Carnac continues to inspire scholarly investigation and popular fascination.
